summary: Yegge provides a comprehensive guide to interviewing at Google, acknowledging upfront that his advice will upset people who don't know the topics he recommends studying. He explains the concept of the 'Interview Anti-Loop' - the reality that every employee has a set of coworkers who wouldn't have hired them, making interviews inherently random. The post covers both mental preparation (stay humble, don't question the interviewer's questions) and technical preparation (algorithms, data structures, graphs, Big-O, operating systems). He emphasizes that Google's high false-negative rate means rejection doesn't reflect your actual abilities, and encourages candidates to reapply after 6-12 months if they don't succeed initially.
key_points: Every employee has an 'Interview Anti-Loop' - a set of coworkers who would not have hired them, making interviews inherently flawed and somewhat random
Google intentionally has a high false-negative rate, preferring to reject qualified candidates over hiring unqualified ones
You cannot tell interviewers how to interview - they believe they're good at it after about one year and never change their methods
Technical preparation should focus on algorithms, data structures (especially graphs and hashtables), Big-O complexity, and concurrency fundamentals
Warm up before interviews like an athlete: study for weeks beforehand and practice intensely the morning of the interview
money_quotes: Everyone who applied had the same reasons for not applying as you do. And everyone here says: 'GOSH, I SURE AM HAPPY I CAME HERE!'
That's almost always what happens when you get an offer from a tech company. You just happened to squeak by.
A and B would probably not even hire each other, given a chance, but they both happened to go through interviewer C.
Hashtables are arguably the single most important data structure known to mankind.
Whenever someone gives you a problem, think graphs. They are the most fundamental and flexible way of representing any kind of a relationship.
The best way to appear arrogant is to question the validity of the interviewer's question – it really ticks them off.
key_insight: Tech interviews are inherently flawed and random due to interviewer bias and the 'Anti-Loop' phenomenon, so rejection doesn't reflect your abilities - prepare thoroughly, stay humble, and try again if you fail.
